exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing. 
In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city.  Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 1 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student:
Let’s work on your **Geographic & Demographic Data Table** for your case study city.

To get it done quickly, tell me:
1) **Which city** you chose (and country/region), and  
2) Whether you want **latest available year** (recommended) or a **specific year**.

Once I have that, I’ll help you fill the table category-by-category (population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, GDP, Köppen climate zone) and make sure each entry has a clean source you can cite.
exchanges.tutor:
Before we fill anything in: what city did you choose (city + country/region), and are you using “latest available” or a specific year?

Also: what source do you *plan* to use as your primary population reference (e.g., national statistics office, census bureau, UN/WorldPop)? Paste the link you found so we can sanity-check it before you build the table.

deductions.reason: Given the planned single exchange, the tutor could have provided a small, structured template for the student to fill (e.g., table headings + where to look), rather than only asking intake questions.
Response is clear and concise, but could be slightly more immediately actionable by listing the table categories it will fill next (as a quick checklist) given the one-turn constraint.
